```text
894	
895	@then(
896	    parsers.parse(
897	        'the BC\'s inbox file contains a gherkin string with a line '
898	        'containing "{needle}"'
899	    )
900	)
901	def inbox_file_gherkin_contains(bc_root: Path, needle: str, context: dict) -> None:
902	    # The CLI must have succeeded; the inbox should now hold a single
903	    # YAML file produced by the send command. Asserting CLI success here
904	    # keeps this Then usable as the only post-send assertion (no separate
905	    # "inbox contains a file named" step needed in this scenario).
906	    rc = context.get("cli_returncode")
907	    assert rc == 0, (
908	        f"shop-msg exited {rc}; stderr:\n{context.get('cli_stderr', '')}"
909	    )
910	    inbox = bc_root / "inbox"
911	    yaml_files = list(inbox.glob("*.yaml"))
912	    assert len(yaml_files) == 1, (
913	        f"expected exactly one inbox yaml after send; found {[p.name for p in yaml_files]}"
914	    )
915	    with yaml_files[0].open() as f:
916	        data = yaml.safe_load(f)
917	    scenarios_field = data.get("scenarios") or []
918	    assert scenarios_field, (
919	        f"expected the inbox payload to carry at least one scenario; got {data!r}"
920	    )
921	    # The "line containing" wording matches the historical gherkin tag
922	    # convention (tags live on their own line). Splitting on newlines
923	    # makes the assertion match that intent literally rather than a
924	    # naive substring search that would tolerate the tag accidentally
925	    # being concatenated mid-step.
926	    found = False
927	    for sp in scenarios_field:
928	        gherkin = sp.get("gherkin", "")
929	        for line in gherkin.splitlines():
930	            if needle in line:
931	                found = True
932	                break
933	        if found:
934	            break
935	    assert found, (
936	        f"expected some scenario's gherkin to contain a line with {needle!r}; "
937	        f"scenarios: {scenarios_field!r}"
938	    )
939	
940	
941	@then(
942	    parsers.re(
943	        r'the BC\'s inbox file "(?P<filename>[^"]*)" parses as a valid '
944	        r'RequestBugfix with description "(?P<description>[^"]*)" '
945	        r'and one scenario whose hash equals the scenarios-hash of the body$'
946	    )
947	)
948	def inbox_file_parses_as_request_bugfix_one_scenario(
949	    bc_root: Path, filename: str, description: str, context: dict
950	) -> None:
951	    # This Then combines "inbox contains the file" and "file parses as ..."
952	    # into a single step (the lead-013 scenario phrases it that way), so it
953	    # has to assert CLI success itself rather than rely on the standalone
954	    # "inbox contains a file named" step.
955	    rc = context.get("cli_returncode")
956	    assert rc == 0, (
957	        f"shop-msg exited {rc}; stderr:\n{context.get('cli_stderr', '')}"
958	    )
959	    path = bc_root / "inbox" / filename
960	    assert path.exists(), (
961	        f"expected {path} to exist; inbox contents: {list((bc_root / 'inbox').iterdir())}"
962	    )
963	    with path.open() as f:
964	        data = yaml.safe_load(f)
965	    # Reaching RequestBugfix(**data) without raising means each embedded
966	    # ScenarioPayload satisfies the schema-level
967	    # `hash == canonical_scenario_hash(gherkin)` invariant lead-018 added.
968	    msg = RequestBugfix(**data)
969	    assert msg.description == description
970	    assert len(msg.scenarios) == 1, (
971	        f"expected exactly one scenario in payload; got {len(msg.scenarios)}"
972	    )
973	    body = context["scenario_body_files"][0]["body"]
974	    actual_hash = msg.scenarios[0].hash
975	    gherkin = msg.scenarios[0].gherkin
976	    # Body must still appear in the emitted gherkin; same reasoning as
977	    # the assign_scenarios Then-step above.
978	    first_body_line = next(
979	        (l for l in body.splitlines() if l.strip()), ""
980	    )
981	    assert first_body_line in gherkin, (
982	        f"expected the body's first non-blank line {first_body_line!r} "
983	        f"to appear in the gherkin; got gherkin:\n{gherkin}"
984	    )
985	    expected_hash = _scenario_hash_via_cli(gherkin)
986	    assert actual_hash == expected_hash, (
987	        f"scenario hash mismatch: CLI emitted {actual_hash!r}, "
988	        f"`scenarios hash` of gherkin produces {expected_hash!r}"
989	    )
990	
991	
992	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
993	# lead-018: hash↔body schema invariant
994	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
995	#
996	# The three scenarios below exercise the schema-level invariant that
997	# `ScenarioPayload.hash == canonical_scenario_hash(gherkin)`. The first
998	# two go directly through the Pydantic constructor (catalog package);
999	# the third drives the same invariant end-to-end through the `shop-msg
1000	# send assign_scenarios` CLI to confirm the producer remains internally
1001	# consistent with the schema.
1002	
1003	
1004	@given(
1005	    parsers.parse(
1006	        'a gherkin body that contains a "{bc_token}" tag line'
1007	    ),
1008	    target_fixture="gherkin_body",
1009	)
1010	def given_gherkin_body_with_bc_tag(bc_token: str) -> str:
1011	    # Construct a small but well-formed gherkin body whose first line is
1012	    # the requested @bc tag token. The body shape mirrors what the rest
1013	    # of the suite uses so any future scenario referencing this Given
1014	    # gets a recognizable shape.
1015	    return (
1016	        f"{bc_token}\n"
1017	        f"Scenario: hash-matches-body construction\n"
1018	        f"    Given a well-formed scenario body\n"
1019	        f"    When I hash the body canonically\n"
1020	        f"    Then the resulting payload validates\n"
1021	    )
1022	
1023	
1024	@given(
1025	    "a hash value equal to the canonical scenario-hash of that gherkin",
1026	    target_fixture="hash_value",
1027	)
1028	def given_matching_hash(gherkin_body: str) -> str:
1029	    # Compute the hash via the same CLI boundary the production code
1030	    # uses (subprocess to `scenarios hash`). This deliberately exercises
1031	    # the cross-package canonicalization rule rather than recomputing
1032	    # via the catalog's internal duplicate, so a drift on either side
1033	    # surfaces here.
1034	    return _scenario_hash_via_cli(gherkin_body)
1035	
1036	
1037	@given(
1038	    "a hash value that does not equal the canonical scenario-hash of that gherkin",
1039	    target_fixture="hash_value",
1040	)
1041	def given_mismatched_hash(gherkin_body: str) -> str:
1042	    # A fixed all-zeros hash is guaranteed non-colliding with any
1043	    # sha256-derived 16-hex-char output for realistic bodies (and the
1044	    # scenario_payload tests sanity-check this assumption).
1045	    wrong = "0000000000000000"
1046	    canonical = _scenario_hash_via_cli(gherkin_body)
1047	    assert wrong != canonical, (
1048	        f"wrong hash {wrong!r} collided with canonical hash; pick another"
1049	    )
1050	    return wrong
1051	
1052	
1053	@when(
1054	    "I construct a ScenarioPayload with that hash and that gherkin",
1055	)
1056	def when_construct_scenario_payload(
1057	    gherkin_body: str, hash_value: str, context: dict
1058	) -> None:
1059	    # Happy-path construction: no expectation of ValidationError, so
1060	    # let any pydantic exception propagate and fail the test loudly.
1061	    payload = ScenarioPayload(hash=hash_value, gherkin=gherkin_body)
1062	    context["scenario_payload"] = payload
1063	
1064	
1065	@when(
1066	    "I construct a ScenarioPayload with that hash and that gherkin via Pydantic",
1067	)
1068	def when_construct_scenario_payload_expecting_error(
1069	    gherkin_body: str, hash_value: str, context: dict
1070	) -> None:
1071	    # Sad-path construction: capture the ValidationError so the Then
1072	    # steps can inspect it. Not raising here would make the rejection
1073	    # check silently incorrect; we assert the raise explicitly in the
1074	    # Then-step below.
1075	    try:
1076	        ScenarioPayload(hash=hash_value, gherkin=gherkin_body)
1077	    except ValidationError as exc:
1078	        context["validation_error"] = exc
1079	        return
1080	    context["validation_error"] = None
1081	
1082	
1083	@then(
1084	    "construction succeeds and the parsed model has the gherkin and hash intact",
1085	)
1086	def then_construction_succeeds(
1087	    gherkin_body: str, hash_value: str, context: dict
1088	) -> None:
1089	    payload: ScenarioPayload = context["scenario_payload"]
1090	    assert payload.gherkin == gherkin_body
1091	    assert payload.hash == hash_value
1092	
1093	
1094	@then("Pydantic raises ValidationError")
1095	def then_pydantic_raises_validation_error(context: dict) -> None:
1096	    exc = context.get("validation_error")
1097	    assert exc is not None, (
1098	        "expected ScenarioPayload(...) to raise ValidationError; "
1099	        "construction returned successfully"
1100	    )
1101	    assert isinstance(exc, ValidationError), (
1102	        f"expected ValidationError; got {type(exc).__name__}: {exc!r}"
1103	    )
1104	
1105	
1106	@then(
1107	    "the error message identifies that the hash does not match the gherkin body",
1108	)
1109	def then_error_identifies_hash_mismatch(context: dict) -> None:
1110	    exc: ValidationError = context["validation_error"]
1111	    msg = str(exc)
1112	    # The diagnostic must name the field-name "hash" so a caller
1113	    # debugging a hand-rolled payload sees which side is wrong, and
1114	    # mention the canonical-mismatch wording so the cause is clear.
1115	    assert "hash" in msg, f"expected error to mention hash; got:\n{msg}"
1116	    assert "canonical" in msg or "does not match" in msg, (
1117	        f"expected error to explain the mismatch; got:\n{msg}"
1118	    )
1119	
1120	
1121	@given(
1122	    "a scenario body file containing well-formed Gherkin steps",
1123	    target_fixture="bc_root_and_body_path",
1124	)
1125	def given_scenario_body_file_for_cli(tmp_path: Path) -> tuple[Path, Path]:
1126	    # The scenario-3 round-trip needs both a BC root (where shop-msg
1127	    # send writes the inbox YAML) and a scenario body file (the input).
1128	    # Returning both via one target fixture keeps the subsequent steps
1129	    # decoupled from the empty_bc / scenario_body_files context the
1130	    # other CLI-driven scenarios share.
1131	    bc_root = tmp_path / "bc"
1132	    (bc_root / "inbox").mkdir(parents=True)
1133	    (bc_root / "outbox").mkdir()
1134	    body = (
1135	        "Scenario: hash-matches-body round-trip\n"
1136	        "    Given a well-formed scenario body\n"
1137	        "    When I send it through shop-msg send assign_scenarios\n"
1138	        "    Then the resulting payload validates against the schema\n"
1139	    )
1140	    body_path = tmp_path / "body.txt"
1141	    body_path.write_text(body)
1142	    return bc_root, body_path
1143	
1144	
1145	@when(
1146	    parsers.parse(
1147	        'I invoke "{cli_phrase}" with that scenario file'
1148	    )
1149	)
1150	def when_invoke_shopmsg_send_assign_scenarios(
1151	    cli_phrase: str, bc_root_and_body_path: tuple[Path, Path], context: dict
1152	) -> None:
1153	    # cli_phrase is the quoted CLI invocation from the feature step. We
1154	    # pin it to "shop-msg send assign_scenarios" so an accidental edit
1155	    # of the feature text to invoke a different command would surface
1156	    # here rather than silently exercising the wrong CLI path.
1157	    assert cli_phrase == "shop-msg send assign_scenarios", (
1158	        f"this step only handles 'shop-msg send assign_scenarios'; got {cli_phrase!r}"
1159	    )
1160	    bc_root, body_path = bc_root_and_body_path
1161	    result = subprocess.run(
1162	        [
1163	            "shop-msg",
1164	            "send",
1165	            "assign_scenarios",
1166	            "--bc-root",
1167	            str(bc_root),
1168	            "--work-id",
1169	            "lead-018-roundtrip",
1170	            "--feature-title",
1171	            "hash matches body round-trip",
1172	            "--bc-tag",
1173	            "shop-msg",
1174	            "--scenario-file",
1175	            str(body_path),
1176	        ],
1177	        capture_output=True,
1178	        text=True,
1179	    )
1180	    context["cli_returncode"] = result.returncode
1181	    context["cli_stdout"] = result.stdout
1182	    context["cli_stderr"] = result.stderr
1183	    context["bc_root_roundtrip"] = bc_root
1184	
1185	
1186	@then("the resulting inbox YAML deserializes into an AssignScenarios message")
1187	def then_inbox_yaml_deserializes(context: dict) -> None:
1188	    rc = context["cli_returncode"]
1189	    assert rc == 0, (
1190	        f"shop-msg exited {rc}; stderr:\n{context['cli_stderr']}"
1191	    )
1192	    bc_root: Path = context["bc_root_roundtrip"]
1193	    inbox_path = bc_root / "inbox" / "lead-018-roundtrip.yaml"
1194	    assert inbox_path.exists(), (
1195	        f"expected {inbox_path} to exist; inbox: {list((bc_root / 'inbox').iterdir())}"
1196	    )
1197	    with inbox_path.open() as f:
1198	        data = yaml.safe_load(f)
1199	    # AssignScenarios(**data) re-validates the entire payload through
1200	    # the Pydantic schema, which means every embedded ScenarioPayload
1201	    # re-runs the hash↔body invariant. A drift would raise here.
1202	    msg = AssignScenarios(**data)
1203	    context["roundtrip_message"] = msg
1204	
1205	
1206	@then(
1207	    "each ScenarioPayload in that message satisfies the schema-level "
1208	    "hash-matches-body invariant"
1209	)
1210	def then_each_payload_satisfies_invariant(context: dict) -> None:
1211	    msg: AssignScenarios = context["roundtrip_message"]
1212	    assert msg.scenarios, "expected at least one scenario in the round-trip message"
1213	    for sp in msg.scenarios:
1214	        # Reaching this point already implies pydantic accepted the
1215	        # payload (the AssignScenarios re-validation in the previous
1216	        # Then). Re-run the canonical hash via the CLI boundary to pin
1217	        # the equality explicitly — a regression in the producer that
1218	        # somehow bypassed the schema (e.g. constructing via
1219	        # model_construct) would surface here.
1220	        expected = _scenario_hash_via_cli(sp.gherkin)
1221	        assert sp.hash == expected, (
1222	            f"round-trip payload violates hash↔body invariant: "
1223	            f"hash={sp.hash!r}, canonical(gherkin)={expected!r}"
1224	        )
1225	
1226	
1227	@when(
1228	    parsers.re(
1229	        r'I run shop-msg respond mechanism_observation with work-id '
1230	        r'"(?P<work_id>[^"]*)" and subject "(?P<subject>[^"]*)" and '
1231	        r'body "(?P<body>[^"]*)"'
1232	    )
1233	)
1234	def run_respond_mechanism_observation(
1235	    bc_root: Path, work_id: str, subject: str, body: str, context: dict
1236	) -> None:
1237	    # After lead-231 item C, mechanism_observation no longer carries a
1238	    # beads-shape bd_ref. The CLI's filename comes from --work-id, like
1239	    # its respond clarify / respond work_done siblings; the schema's
1240	    # optional provenance_ref (when supplied) is the BC's tracker-
1241	    # neutral pointer to a long-form record. The pre-existing scenarios
1242	    # exercise only the filename + subject + body path, so this When-
1243	    # step does not pass --provenance-ref.
1244	    result = subprocess.run(
1245	        [
1246	            "shop-msg", "respond", "mechanism_observation",
1247	            "--bc-root", str(bc_root),
1248	            "--work-id", work_id,
1249	            "--subject", subject,
1250	            "--body", body,
1251	        ],
1252	        capture_output=True,
1253	        text=True,
1254	    )
1255	    context["cli_returncode"] = result.returncode
1256	    context["cli_stdout"] = result.stdout
1257	    context["cli_stderr"] = result.stderr
1258	
1259	
1260	@then(
1261	    parsers.re(
1262	        r'the file parses as a valid MechanismObservation with '
1263	        r'work_id "(?P<work_id>[^"]*)" and subject "(?P<subject>[^"]*)"'
1264	    )
1265	)
1266	def file_parses_as_mechanism_observation(
1267	    bc_root: Path, work_id: str, subject: str, context: dict
1268	) -> None:
1269	    # work_id is the filename key (not a schema field on
1270	    # MechanismObservation, which carries no work_id); the schema
1271	    # invariant is checked by model_validate succeeding.
1272	    path = bc_root / "outbox" / f"{work_id}-mechanism_observation.yaml"
1273	    raw = yaml.safe_load(path.read_text())
1274	    obs = MechanismObservation.model_validate(raw)
1275	    assert obs.subject == subject
1276	
1277	
1278	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
1279	# lead-231.1: pending enumeration and read inbox
1280	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
1281	#
1282	# Step definitions for the seven scenarios in
1283	# features/pending_and_read_inbox.feature. Three message-type families
1284	# are exercised:
1285	#
1286	#   * `shop-msg pending inbox  --bc-root <path>` (BC-side queries)
1287	#   * `shop-msg pending outbox --lead-root <path> [--bc <name>]`
1288	#     (lead-side queries across sibling BC clones)
1289	#   * `shop-msg read inbox     --bc-root <path> --work-id <id>`
1290	#
1291	# The Given-steps drive the same production CLI surfaces (send /
1292	# respond) to set up state, so any regression in the producer side
1293	# would surface here rather than being hidden behind hand-rolled YAML.
1294	
1295	
1296	def _shop_msg_send_inbox(bc_root: Path, message_type: str, work_id: str) -> None:
1297	    """Drive `shop-msg send <message_type>` for the pending-listing tests.
1298	
1299	    The pending-listing scenarios care that an inbox file exists and
1300	    that its parsed message_type matches; they do not pin the payload
1301	    beyond that. So this helper picks a minimum-viable payload for each
1302	    of the three lead-to-BC message types and shells out to the same
1303	    production CLI a real lead-shop would use.
1304	    """
1305	    if message_type == "request_maintenance":
1306	        cmd = [
1307	            "shop-msg", "send", "request_maintenance",
1308	            "--bc-root", str(bc_root),
1309	            "--work-id", work_id,
1310	            "--description", "pending-listing setup payload",
1311	        ]
1312	    elif message_type == "request_bugfix":
1313	        cmd = [
1314	            "shop-msg", "send", "request_bugfix",
1315	            "--bc-root", str(bc_root),
1316	            "--work-id", work_id,
1317	            "--description", "pending-listing setup payload",
1318	        ]
1319	    elif message_type == "assign_scenarios":
1320	        # assign_scenarios requires at least one --scenario-file; build
1321	        # a minimal scenario body in a sibling tmp file. The body needs
1322	        # to be plausible Gherkin so the producer's hash-and-wrap path
1323	        # succeeds. We put the body file inside the BC root's parent so
1324	        # it's bounded to the same tmp_path lifetime.
1325	        body_path = bc_root.parent / f"_pending_body_{work_id}.txt"
1326	        body_path.write_text(
1327	            "Scenario: pending-listing setup\n"
1328	            "    Given an inbox setup body\n"
1329	            "    When the BC receives it\n"
1330	            "    Then it is parsed as a valid AssignScenarios\n"
1331	        )
1332	        cmd = [
1333	            "shop-msg", "send", "assign_scenarios",
1334	            "--bc-root", str(bc_root),
1335	            "--work-id", work_id,
1336	            "--feature-title", "pending-listing setup",
1337	            "--bc-tag", "shopsystem-messaging",
1338	            "--scenario-file", str(body_path),
1339	        ]
1340	    else:
1341	        raise AssertionError(f"unhandled message_type in setup helper: {message_type!r}")
1342	    subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, check=True)
1343	
1344	
1345	@given(
1346	    parsers.parse(
1347	        'shop-msg send assign_scenarios was previously used to write an '
1348	        'inbox message with work-id "{work_id}"'
1349	    )
1350	)
1351	def given_prior_inbox_assign_scenarios(bc_root: Path, work_id: str) -> None:
1352	    _shop_msg_send_inbox(bc_root, "assign_scenarios", work_id)
1353	
1354	
1355	@given(
1356	    parsers.parse(
1357	        'shop-msg send request_bugfix was previously used to write an '
1358	        'inbox message with work-id "{work_id}"'
1359	    )
1360	)
1361	def given_prior_inbox_request_bugfix(bc_root: Path, work_id: str) -> None:
1362	    _shop_msg_send_inbox(bc_root, "request_bugfix", work_id)
1363	
1364	
1365	@given(

1381	def given_prior_outbox_work_done(bc_root: Path, work_id: str, status: str) -> None:

1399	@given(
1400	    parsers.parse(
1401	        'shop-msg send assign_scenarios was previously used to write an '

1406	def given_prior_inbox_assign_scenarios_tagged(
1407	    bc_root: Path, work_id: str, bc_tag: str, context: dict
1408	) -> None:
1409	    # The read-inbox happy-path scenario also wants to assert that the
1410	    # gherkin body appears in stdout, so we capture the body we feed in
1411	    # for the matching Then-step to compare against.
1412	    suffix = bc_tag.removeprefix("@bc:") if bc_tag.startswith("@bc:") else bc_tag
1413	    body_path = bc_root.parent / f"_read_body_{work_id}.txt"
1414	    body_text = (
1415	        "Scenario: read-inbox happy-path setup\n"

1417	        "    When the BC reads its inbox\n"

1419	    )

1421	    subprocess.run(

1424	            "--bc-root", str(bc_root),
1425	            "--work-id", work_id,
1426	            "--feature-title", "read-inbox happy-path setup",
1427	            "--bc-tag", suffix,
1428	            "--scenario-file", str(body_path),
1429	        ],
1430	        capture_output=True,
1431	        text=True,
1432	        check=True,
1433	    )
1434	    context["read_inbox_body_text"] = body_text

1437	@given(
1438	    parsers.parse(
1439	        'the BC\'s inbox already contains a file for work-id "{work_id}" '

1443	def given_inbox_invalid_lead_message(
1444	    bc_root: Path, work_id: str
1445	) -> None:
1446	    # Valid YAML (parses to a dict) but the message_type is not one of
1447	    # the discriminated-union branches of LeadMessage, so the schema
1448	    # rejection in `read inbox` routes through the validation-failed
1449	    # branch. Mirrors the existing outbox-invalid setup step
1450	    # `outbox_preexisting_invalid_response` so the two error paths stay
1451	    # symmetric.
1452	    path = bc_root / "inbox" / f"{work_id}.yaml"
1453	    path.write_text(
1454	        "message_type: not_a_real_type\n"
1455	        f"work_id: {work_id}\n"
1456	        "description: this payload is structurally valid YAML\n"
1457	    )

1460	@given(
1461	    parsers.parse(
1462	        'a lead shop at a temporary path with BC clones "{bc_a}" and '
1463	        '"{bc_b}" present as sibling directories'
1464	    ),
1465	    target_fixture="lead_root",
1466	)
1467	def given_lead_shop_with_two_bcs(
1468	    tmp_path: Path, bc_a: str, bc_b: str
1469	) -> Path:
1470	    # Lead-side layout mirrors the production shape: a `repos/` dir
1471	    # holds sibling BC clones, each with its own inbox/outbox. The
1472	    # pending-outbox scenarios only need the outbox to exist (the
1473	    # Given-step that follows will populate it via the CLI).
1474	    lead_root = tmp_path / "lead"
1475	    repos = lead_root / "repos"
1476	    repos.mkdir(parents=True)
1477	    for name in (bc_a, bc_b):
1478	        bc = repos / name
1479	        (bc / "inbox").mkdir(parents=True)
1480	        (bc / "outbox").mkdir()
1481	    return lead_root

1530	@when(
1531	    "I run the shop-msg subcommand that enumerates pending unprocessed "
1532	    "inbox messages, with no filter"
1533	)
1534	def run_pending_inbox(bc_root: Path, context: dict) -> None:
1535	    # The "no filter" wording in the scenario is satisfied by the
1536	    # `pending inbox` subcommand having no required scoping flag beyond
1537	    # --bc-root. The scenario also asserts the caller did not have to
1538	    # inspect inbox/outbox directly; running the subcommand at all
1539	    # satisfies that, but we record the argv for the matching Then-step
1540	    # to inspect.
1541	    result = subprocess.run(
1542	        [
1543	            "shop-msg", "pending", "inbox",
1544	            "--bc-root", str(bc_root),
1545	        ],
1546	        capture_output=True,
1547	        text=True,
1548	    )
1549	    context["cli_returncode"] = result.returncode
1550	    context["cli_stdout"] = result.stdout
1551	    context["cli_stderr"] = result.stderr
1552	    context["cli_argv"] = ["shop-msg", "pending", "inbox", "--bc-root", str(bc_root)]
1553	
1554

1556	    parsers.parse(

1559	    )
1560	)

1596	@then("stdout contains no work_id entries")
1597	def stdout_no_work_id_entries(context: dict) -> None:
1598	    # "no work_id entries" means the listing produced zero entries —
1599	    # the output is empty (or whitespace-only). The pending CLI prints
1600	    # one line per entry, so an empty listing is "no non-blank lines".
1601	    stdout = context.get("cli_stdout", "")
1602	    nonblank = [line for line in stdout.splitlines() if line.strip()]
1603	    assert nonblank == [], (
1604	        f"expected no work_id entries; got lines:\n{nonblank}"
1605	    )
1606	
1607	
1608	@then("the command did not require the caller to inspect the inbox or outbox directories")
1609	def command_did_not_require_caller_directory_inspection(context: dict) -> None:
1610	    # This Then-step is observability over the CLI contract: the caller
1611	    # ran a single shop-msg invocation and did not need to walk the
1612	    # mailbox directories itself. Concretely we assert (a) the When-step
1613	    # invoked the subcommand (cli_returncode is set), and (b) the argv
1614	    # captured by the When-step references no path under inbox/ or
1615	    # outbox/ — only --bc-root pointing at the BC root.
1616	    assert "cli_returncode" in context, (
1617	        "expected the pending-inbox subcommand to have been invoked; "
1618	        "the When-step did not record a cli_returncode"
1619	    )
1620	    argv = context.get("cli_argv", [])
1621	    for arg in argv:
1622	        # The caller may legitimately pass paths whose names contain
1623	        # 'inbox' or 'outbox' as substrings (e.g. a directory named
1624	        # 'pinbox'), so we check for the actual mailbox subpaths.
1625	        assert "/inbox/" not in arg and not arg.endswith("/inbox"), (
1626	            f"expected argv to not point at inbox/; got {arg!r}"
1627	        )
1628	        assert "/outbox/" not in arg and not arg.endswith("/outbox"), (
1629	            f"expected argv to not point at outbox/; got {arg!r}"
1630	        )
1631	
1632	
1633	def _stdout_lines(context: dict) -> list[str]:
1634	    return [
1635	        line for line in context.get("cli_stdout", "").splitlines() if line.strip()
1636	    ]
1637	
1638	
1639	@then(
1640	    parsers.re(
1641	        r'stdout includes an entry for work_id "(?P<work_id>[^"]*)" '
1642	        r'with message_type "(?P<message_type>[^"]*)"$'
1643	    )
1644	)
1645	def stdout_includes_pending_entry(
1646	    context: dict, work_id: str, message_type: str
1647	) -> None:
1648	    # Pending-listing entries are one per line; each line has the
1649	    # work_id token and the message_type token among its whitespace-
1650	    # separated fields. We split on whitespace rather than substring-
1651	    # check so a stray substring (e.g. a message_type that happens to
1652	    # contain a work_id) cannot accidentally satisfy the assertion.
1653	    for line in _stdout_lines(context):
1654	        tokens = line.split()
1655	        if work_id in tokens and message_type in tokens:
1656	            return
1657	    raise AssertionError(
1658	        f"expected an entry matching work_id={work_id!r} and "
1659	        f"message_type={message_type!r}; lines:\n{_stdout_lines(context)}"
1660	    )
1661	
1662	
1663	@then(
1664	    parsers.parse(
1665	        'stdout contains no entry for work_id "{work_id}"'
1666	    )
1667	)
1668	def stdout_no_entry_for_work_id(context: dict, work_id: str) -> None:
1669	    for line in _stdout_lines(context):
1670	        tokens = line.split()
1671	        assert work_id not in tokens, (
1672	            f"expected no entry for {work_id!r}; found line: {line!r}"
1673	        )
1674	
1675	
1676	@then(
1677	    parsers.re(
1678	        r'stdout includes an entry for work_id "(?P<work_id>[^"]*)" '
1679	        r'with message_type "(?P<message_type>[^"]*)" originating from '
1680	        r'BC "(?P<bc>[^"]*)"$'
1681	    )
1682	)
1683	def stdout_includes_pending_outbox_entry(
1684	    context: dict, work_id: str, message_type: str, bc: str
1685	) -> None:
1686	    # Lead-side listing entries carry the originating BC name as a
1687	    # third token. Split-and-check keeps the assertion robust against
1688	    # incidental substring overlap (e.g. a message_type that happens
1689	    # to share characters with the BC name).
1690	    for line in _stdout_lines(context):
1691	        tokens = line.split()
1692	        if work_id in tokens and message_type in tokens and bc in tokens:
1693	            return
1694	    raise AssertionError(
1695	        f"expected an entry matching work_id={work_id!r}, "
1696	        f"message_type={message_type!r}, bc={bc!r}; lines:\n"
1697	        f"{_stdout_lines(context)}"
1698	    )
1699	
1700	
1701	@then("stdout includes the gherkin body of the ScenarioPayload that was sent")
1702	def stdout_includes_gherkin_body(context: dict) -> None:
1703	    # The read-inbox setup step captured the body text it fed into
1704	    # `shop-msg send assign_scenarios`. The CLI wraps that body with a
1705	    # Feature header and tags before hashing, and dumps the wrapped
1706	    # form on `read inbox`. So the body's first non-blank line is
1707	    # what we assert appears in stdout — same shape as the existing
1708	    # `inbox_file_gherkin_contains` step's intent.
1709	    body_text = context.get("read_inbox_body_text")
1710	    assert body_text is not None, (
1711	        "expected the setup step to have captured the body it sent; "
1712	        "no read_inbox_body_text in context"
1713	    )
1714	    first_body_line = next(
1715	        (l for l in body_text.splitlines() if l.strip()), ""
1716	    )
1717	    stdout = context.get("cli_stdout", "")
1718	    assert first_body_line in stdout, (
1719	        f"expected stdout to contain the body's first non-blank line "
1720	        f"{first_body_line!r}; stdout:\n{stdout}"
1721	    )
1722	
1723	
1724	@then("stderr explains no inbox message was found for that work_id")
1725	def stderr_explains_no_inbox_message(context: dict) -> None:
1726	    # Substring-check on the salient phrase the CLI uses on miss; same
1727	    # pattern as `stderr_explains_no_outbox_response` so a future
1728	    # consolidation can fold both branches without drifting one of
1729	    # them.
1730	    stderr = context.get("cli_stderr", "")
1731	    assert "no inbox message" in stderr, (
1732	        f"expected stderr to explain no inbox message was found; got:\n{stderr}"
1733	    )
1734	
1735	
1736	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
1737	# lead-231.2: catalog schema bd-decoupling
1738	# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
1739	#
1740	# Six scenarios pinning the invariant that none of the six
1741	# LeadMessage/BCResponse schemas requires a beads identifier as a field.
1742	# Each scenario is a Given/When/Then triple of the same shape across the
1743	# six message types, so we register one parametrized set of steps that
1744	# dispatches on the schema name carried in the step text. The
1745	# discriminator is the class name itself (e.g. "AssignScenarios"),
1746	# pulled from the @given step that introduces each scenario.
1747	
1748	# Minimal-required-field payloads for each of the six schemas. The set
1749	# of keys is exactly the required fields — no extras — so that the
1750	# "supplying only the fields the schema marks as required" wording in
1751	# the gherkin is satisfied literally. The values are placeholder
1752	# strings of the right shape; the scenarios assert construction
1753	# succeeds, not that any particular field has any particular value.
1754	#
1755	# RequestMaintenance: required = message_type, work_id, description
1756	# AssignScenarios:    required = message_type, work_id, scenarios (list)
1757	# RequestBugfix:      required = message_type, work_id, description
1758	# Clarify:            required = message_type, work_id (pattern), question
1759	# WorkDone:           required = message_type, work_id, status (enum)
1760	# MechanismObservation:
1761	#                     required = message_type, subject (>=5 chars),
1762	#                     body (>=50 chars)
1763	# The catalog has no required field whose name begins with "bd_" or
1764	# otherwise names a beads identifier — which is precisely the property
1765	# these scenarios pin.
1766	_MINIMAL_REQUIRED_PAYLOADS: dict[str, dict] = {
1767	    "AssignScenarios": {
1768	        "message_type": "assign_scenarios",
1769	        "work_id": "lead-bd-decoupling",
1770	        "scenarios": [],
1771	    },
1772	    "RequestBugfix": {
1773	        "message_type": "request_bugfix",
1774	        "work_id": "lead-bd-decoupling",
1775	        "description": "minimal fixture description",
1776	    },
1777	    "RequestMaintenance": {
1778	        "message_type": "request_maintenance",
1779	        "work_id": "lead-bd-decoupling",
1780	        "description": "minimal fixture description",
1781	    },
1782	    "Clarify": {
1783	        "message_type": "clarify",
1784	        "work_id": "lead-bd-decoupling",
1785	        "question": "minimal fixture question",
1786	    },
1787	    "WorkDone": {
1788	        "message_type": "work_done",
1789	        "work_id": "lead-bd-decoupling",
1790	        "status": "complete",
1791	    },
1792	    "MechanismObservation": {
1793	        "message_type": "mechanism_observation",
1794	        "subject": "minimal subject",
1795	        # body min_length is 50; pad the fixture text to that length.
1796	        "body": (
1797	            "Minimal mechanism observation body text padded to the "
1798	            "schema-required minimum of fifty characters."
1799	        ),
1800	    },
1801	}
1802	
1803	
1804	def _schema_class_for(name: str):
1805	    """Resolve a schema class by its bare class name.
1806	
1807	    Importing the classes inline (rather than module-level) keeps the
1808	    dispatch local to the steps that need it and makes the dependency on
1809	    the catalog package's surface explicit at the call site.
1810	    """
1811	    from catalog import schemas as _catalog_schemas
1812	
1813	    cls = getattr(_catalog_schemas, name, None)
1814	    assert cls is not None, (
1815	        f"unknown schema class name {name!r}; expected one of "
1816	        f"{sorted(_MINIMAL_REQUIRED_PAYLOADS.keys())}"
1817	    )
1818	    return cls
1819	
1820	
1821	@given(
1822	    parsers.parse("the {schema_name} schema from the shop-msg catalog"),
1823	    target_fixture="bd_decoupling_schema_name",
1824	)
1825	def given_schema_from_catalog(schema_name: str) -> str:
1826	    # The Given-step records which of the six schemas this scenario is
1827	    # exercising. The dispatch table above maps the bare class name to
1828	    # its minimal-required payload; assert the name is one we know how
1829	    # to drive so a typo in the feature surfaces here loudly.
1830	    assert schema_name in _MINIMAL_REQUIRED_PAYLOADS, (
1831	        f"{schema_name!r} is not one of the six bd-decoupled schemas; "
1832	        f"expected one of {sorted(_MINIMAL_REQUIRED_PAYLOADS.keys())}"
1833	    )
1834	    return schema_name
1835	
1836	
1837	# The When-step's text is verbose ("construct an X instance supplying
1838	# only the fields the schema marks as required, with no field whose
1839	# name begins with bd_ ..."). We match it with a regex that pulls out
1840	# the schema class name (which must equal the @given's name so the two
1841	# stay in lockstep) and discards the rest of the prose. A single
1842	# regex covers all six scenarios because the only variable token is
1843	# the class name.
1844	@when(
1845	    parsers.re(
1846	        r'I construct (?:an?|a) (?P<schema_name>[A-Za-z]+) instance '
1847	        r'supplying only the fields the schema marks as required, '
1848	        r'with no field whose name begins with "bd_" or otherwise '
1849	        r'references a beads issue identifier'
1850	    )
1851	)
1852	def when_construct_minimal_instance(
1853	    schema_name: str,
1854	    bd_decoupling_schema_name: str,
1855	    context: dict,
1856	) -> None:
1857	    # Cross-check: the When's class name must equal the Given's. A
1858	    # divergence would mean the feature accidentally pairs a Given for
1859	    # one schema with a When for another, which the schema dispatch
1860	    # would silently paper over.
1861	    assert schema_name == bd_decoupling_schema_name, (
1862	        f"When-step schema {schema_name!r} does not match "
1863	        f"Given-step schema {bd_decoupling_schema_name!r}; the two must "
1864	        f"name the same class"
1865	    )
1866	    cls = _schema_class_for(schema_name)
1867	    payload = _MINIMAL_REQUIRED_PAYLOADS[schema_name]
1868	    # Cross-check the payload at the source: no key may begin with
1869	    # "bd_" or mention "beads", which is the same property the schema
1870	    # itself is asserted to have in the Then-step below. If a future
1871	    # edit accidentally re-introduces a bd_-prefixed key here, the
1872	    # assertion below catches it before the construction even runs.
1873	    for key in payload:
1874	        assert not key.startswith("bd_"), (
1875	            f"fixture payload for {schema_name} introduced key {key!r} "
1876	            f"that begins with 'bd_'; that would defeat the test"
1877	        )
1878	        assert "beads" not in key.lower(), (
1879	            f"fixture payload for {schema_name} introduced key {key!r} "
1880	            f"naming beads; that would defeat the test"
1881	        )
1882	    try:
1883	        instance = cls(**payload)
1884	    except Exception as exc:  # pragma: no cover - the Then asserts non-raise
1885	        context["bd_decoupling_error"] = exc
1886	        context["bd_decoupling_instance"] = None
1887	        return
1888	    context["bd_decoupling_error"] = None
1889	    context["bd_decoupling_instance"] = instance
1890	
1891	
1892	@then("construction succeeds")
1893	def then_construction_succeeds_bd(context: dict) -> None:
1894	    err = context.get("bd_decoupling_error")
1895	    assert err is None, (
1896	        f"expected construction to succeed; got {type(err).__name__}: {err}"
1897	    )
1898	    assert context.get("bd_decoupling_instance") is not None, (
1899	        "expected an instance to be constructed; got None"
1900	    )
1901	
1902	
1903	@then("no schema validation error is raised")
1904	def then_no_validation_error(context: dict) -> None:
1905	    # Sibling of the "construction succeeds" Then-step. Splitting the
1906	    # two keeps the gherkin readable as two distinct assertions (the
1907	    # constructor did not raise; AND no validation error). Both check
1908	    # the same underlying state — that's deliberate; the gherkin
1909	    # phrasing is the lead's, we match it.
1910	    err = context.get("bd_decoupling_error")
1911	    assert err is None, (
1912	        f"expected no validation error; got {type(err).__name__}: {err}"
1913	    )
1914	
1915	
1916	@then(
1917	    "no required field of the schema names a beads identifier "
1918	    "in its name, type, or validation pattern"
1919	)
1920	def then_no_required_field_names_beads(
1921	    bd_decoupling_schema_name: str,
1922	) -> None:
1923	    # Direct schema-introspection check, complementary to the
1924	    # construction-succeeds path: walk the model's required fields and
1925	    # assert none names a beads identifier by field-name, by Python
1926	    # type annotation (which would surface as a custom type whose name
1927	    # mentions beads), or by validation pattern (the previous schema
1928	    # used a beads-shape regex with prefix-and-suffix hyphenation;
1929	    # the new shape, if any, must be neutral).
1930	    cls = _schema_class_for(bd_decoupling_schema_name)
1931	    for name, field in cls.model_fields.items():
1932	        if not field.is_required():
1933	            continue
1934	        # Field-name check.
1935	        assert not name.startswith("bd_"), (
1936	            f"{cls.__name__}.{name} is required and begins with 'bd_'; "
1937	            f"violates lead-231 item C decoupling invariant"
1938	        )
1939	        assert "beads" not in name.lower(), (
1940	            f"{cls.__name__}.{name} is required and names beads; "
1941	            f"violates lead-231 item C decoupling invariant"
1942	        )
1943	        # Type-name check: the annotation's string representation must
1944	        # not name a beads-specific type.
1945	        annotation_str = str(field.annotation).lower()
1946	        assert "beads" not in annotation_str, (
1947	            f"{cls.__name__}.{name} has annotation {field.annotation!r} "
1948	            f"that names beads; violates lead-231 item C"
1949	        )
1950	        # Pattern check: walk the field's metadata for pydantic
1951	        # constraints that carry a beads-shape pattern. The previous
1952	        # bd_ref regex was `^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9-]*-[a-z0-9]+$` — a
1953	        # prefix-hyphen-suffix shape characteristic of beads ids.
1954	        # Asserting the pattern, if any, does NOT mandate that shape
1955	        # keeps the schema neutral.
1956	        for meta in getattr(field, "metadata", []):
1957	            pattern = getattr(meta, "pattern", None)
1958	            if pattern is None:
1959	                continue
1960	            # The beads-id shape requires at least one inner hyphen
1961	            # separating prefix from suffix. A pattern that does not
1962	            # mandate that hyphen is, by construction, not the
1963	            # beads-shape regex.
1964	            assert "-[a-z0-9]+$" not in pattern, (
1965	                f"{cls.__name__}.{name} validation pattern {pattern!r} "
1966	                f"matches the beads issue-id shape; violates lead-231 "
1967	                f"item C decoupling invariant"
1968	            )
1969
```

```text
2	them as pytest-bdd scenarios. No-op when no .feature files are present.

5	
6	from pytest_bdd import scenarios
```

```text
                if os.path.dirname(executable):
                    executable_list = (executable,)

                        os.path.join(os.fsencode(dir), executable)
                        for dir in os.get_exec_path(env))
```

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-15 20:47:52Z)**_

Two things went wrong there:

1. **I jumped ahead.** Your question was meta — "do we have a mechanism?" — which I can answer directly without dispatching. That's conversational clarification, and CLAUDE.md says to handle it in main context.

2. **Reading code is the wrong empirical method anyway.** CLAUDE.md is explicit: "Reading the BC's code is hypothesis; running it is fact." If we do invoke the Architect, the verification should be constructing a concrete input and observing behavior — not a code audit.

To actually answer your question: yes, we have a mechanism. `request_maintenance` is the vehicle for implementation swaps that don't change a BC's external interface. If postgres-as-backend is purely an internal change — same observable behavior, same interface, just different storage — that's the right discriminator and we skip the PO/scenario authoring step entirely.

The open question is whether the swap introduces anything externally observable (e.g. persistence across restarts that didn't exist before). Do you know enough about the current messaging backend to say whether that's the case, or do you want the Architect to find out by running it?

---

_**User (2026-05-15 20:52:10Z)**_

There should not be anything externally observable and the architect should issue a request_maintenance for the BC to implement.

Here are notes from an external analysis on the implementation based on the features:

Yes, Postgres works well for this exact shape of system, and you can do it with the CLI talking directly to the database — no intermediate server needed. Three Postgres features carry the design:

**`UNIQUE` constraints for collision refusal.** Your `work_id` collision rule maps directly. `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING RETURNING id` lets the CLI see whether the insert took, and you exit non-zero when it didn't. Atomic by construction, no race.

**`LISTEN/NOTIFY` for the event-driven agent piece.** This is the key thing for "agents respond to events." After a write, the CLI issues `NOTIFY inbox_<bc>, '<work_id>'`. The agent holds a long-lived connection with `LISTEN inbox_<bc>` and wakes immediately. Notifications aren't durable — if no one is listening when NOTIFY fires, the wake-up is lost — but the row is still in the table, so the agent runs a "drain pending" query on startup before entering the listen loop. That covers missed wakeups cleanly.

**`JSONB` payloads + Pydantic at the boundary.** Store the validated message as JSONB; let Pydantic validate in Python before the insert, exactly as your schemas do today. You can additionally encode invariants like `ScenarioPayload`'s hash-matches-body as a CHECK constraint for defence in depth, but Pydantic at the boundary is probably enough.

Concrete sketch:

```sql
CREATE TABLE messages (
  id           BIGSERIAL   PRIMARY KEY,
  bc           TEXT        NOT NULL,
  work_id      TEXT        NOT NULL,
  direction    TEXT        NOT NULL CHECK (direction IN ('inbox','outbox')),
  message_type TEXT        NOT NULL,
  payload      JSONB       NOT NULL,
  created_at   TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  UNIQUE (bc, work_id, direction, message_type)
);
```

"Pending" becomes a join:

```sql
SELECT i.work_id, i.message_type
FROM messages i
WHERE i.bc = $1 AND i.direction = 'inbox'
  AND NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM messages o
    WHERE o.bc = i.bc AND o.work_id = i.work_id AND o.direction = 'outbox'
  );
```

The `--bc` filter from your `pending_and_read_inbox.feature` is just `WHERE bc = $1`. Read-by-work-id is a trivial SELECT. The schema-validation-failed scenarios become "row's payload can't be parsed into the Pydantic model on read" — same error shape, different storage layer.

For the client library, **psycopg** (v3) gives you everything in one dependency: parameterized statements, JSON marshalling, and async `LISTEN` loops if you want them. Synchronous is fine too if each agent is single-threaded.

**On whether to use a queue library.** `procrastinate` and `pgqueuer` are both Python, both Postgres-native, both use LISTEN/NOTIFY under the hood. They wrap the pattern with general job-queue semantics — retries, scheduling, dead-letter handling. For your case those add abstractions you don't need: your "queue" already has highly specific semantics (work_id collision refusal, hash-matches-body, distinct LeadMessage/BCResponse schemas) that don't map cleanly onto a generic job model. Rolling it directly on psycopg is probably 150–250 lines and stays close to the model you've already specified in features.

**Honest trade-offs:**

Multiple agents per BC need `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` to claim work safely. With one agent per BC (which is what the features suggest) you don't need it at all.

Long-lived LISTEN connections need reconnect handling. Plan for it: on disconnect, reconnect, re-drain pending, then re-LISTEN. This is the one piece of operational care the design demands.

NOTIFY payloads are capped at ~8KB — don't try to ship the whole message in the notification. Send just the `work_id` and let the agent fetch the row.

You lose the easy `cat`/`grep` debugging affordance of files on disk. Mitigate with a `shop-msg dump` subcommand or just keep `psql` handy.

One thing worth calling out from your features: the "lead shop with BC clones as sibling directories" model in `d71fb7f28c4cd561` becomes just `WHERE bc = 'bc-alpha'` — the multi-BC topology gets simpler, not more complex, in the DB model. You no longer need the lead to walk a directory tree.

For your stated scale and the semantics you've already pinned down, this is a good fit and a small change in moving parts: one Postgres instance, no extra server, CLI talks directly via psycopg.

You are the **lead-architect** subagent for the shopsystem lead shop. Read `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/.claude/agents/lead-architect.md` for your full role definition before proceeding.

## Task

Issue a `request_maintenance` to the shopsystem-messaging BC to replace its current storage backend with Postgres.

**Discriminator already applied by the router:**
- The change is an internal implementation swap — no externally observable behavior changes
- `request_maintenance` is the correct vehicle
- Do not re-litigate the discriminator

## Design context (from external analysis — include in the message)

The external analysis confirms Postgres works well for this system shape. Key design points:

- **Schema**: single `messages` table with `(bc, work_id, direction, message_type)` UNIQUE constraint for collision refusal. JSONB payload column. `BIGSERIAL` PK, `TIMESTAMPTZ created_at`.
- **Pending query**: LEFT JOIN / NOT EXISTS between inbox and outbox rows for a given bc — replaces directory walking, simpler multi-BC topology.
- **LISTEN/NOTIFY**: after insert, `NOTIFY inbox_<bc>, '<work_id>'`. Agent holds long-lived LISTEN connection; drains pending on startup before entering listen loop to cover missed notifications.
- **Client library**: psycopg v3 (single dependency; parameterized statements, JSON marshalling, async LISTEN support).
- **No queue library** (procrastinate/pgqueuer): rolling directly on psycopg keeps it close to the existing semantics and avoids generic job-queue abstractions that don't map cleanly.
- **Pydantic at the boundary**: validate before insert, same as today. Optional CHECK constraints for defence in depth.
- **Operational notes**: reconnect + re-drain on LISTEN connection loss; NOTIFY payload is work_id only (not full message, stays under 8KB cap); `shop-msg dump` subcommand or psql for debuggability.
- **Multi-agent**: `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` only needed if multiple agents per BC; features suggest one agent per BC so not required initially.

## Steps

1. Run `bd create` to create a lead beads issue for this work. Use something like:
   - title: "request_maintenance: shopsystem-messaging postgres backend"
   - description: why (current file/yaml storage limits multi-BC topology; postgres simplifies it and adds durability)
   - type: task, priority: 2
   Capture the issue ID (e.g. `lead-xxx`).

2. Run `shop-msg send request_maintenance` targeting shopsystem-messaging with:
   - `--work-id` = the beads issue ID from step 1
   - The maintenance description should be clear: replace current storage backend with Postgres using psycopg v3; no interface changes; design notes above are the implementation guide
   - Include the schema sketch and design notes as the body

   Check `shop-msg send request_maintenance --help` first to see exact flags/format.

3. Report back:
   - The beads issue ID created
   - The exact `shop-msg send` command you ran
   - Whether it succeeded
   - Any output or errors

Working directory: /workspaces/shopsystem-product

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./.claude/agents/lead-architect.md`

```text
1	# Lead Architect — role prompt
2	
3	You are the **Architect** for the lead shop. You own product shape, scenario
4	assignment, and reconciliation. Your job has two faces:
5	
6	1. **Sending work to BCs** — composing `assign_scenarios`, `request_bugfix`,
7	   `request_maintenance`, `request_shop_card`, and `request_scenario_register`
8	   messages from the lead shop.
9	2. **Responding to BC clarify on architecture** — when a BC asks about
10	   structure, contracts, decomposition, or other shape questions, you are
11	   the named party who answers.
12	
13	The procedural CLI mechanics for putting these activities on the wire are
14	deferred to the "CLI mechanics" section near the bottom of this prompt;
15	everything above that section is about what the role *is*, not what
16	command to type.
17	
18	## Your default posture: PRE-STATE DETERMINES VEHICLE — VERIFIED EMPIRICALLY
19	
20	The BC's pre-state determines the message type. **Not the surface impression
21	of the work, not the prior-slice pattern, not "this feels like a bugfix."**
22	The question to ask before every outbound message:
23	
24	> *"Is the BC's pre-state already doing this thing in some unpinned form,
25	> or does it not have the capability at all?"*
26	
27	If the BC has no capability → `assign_scenarios` (lead commits new behavior
28	via Gherkin). If the BC has the behavior but no scenario pins it →
29	`request_bugfix` (lead tightens unpinned existing behavior). If the BC has
30	the behavior and scenarios pin it but the lead wants a flat change with no
31	new scenarios → `request_maintenance`. Pattern-matching on prior slices
32	has been observed to produce wrong-vehicle selection (see
33	`findings-from-prototype-1.md` §5).
34	
35	**The answer to the pre-state question must be empirically verified, not
36	asserted from reading.** Reading code is hypothesis; running it is fact.
37	In rear view the two slip easily into "I already checked"; in advance
38	they diverge. The pre-state verification step is mechanical: construct
39	the concrete input that exhibits the behavior you claim exists (or
40	doesn't), run it, observe the result. Cite the demonstration in the
41	dispatch description so the Implementer does not have to re-discover it.
42	
43	## Your job
44	
45	Your job is the §3.2 Architect activity catalogue, made operational. The
46	§3.2 spec catalogues eight Architect activities. Each is listed below
47	with the one-line guidance that governs it. None of these are placeholders
48	— if a future spec adds an activity for which this template doesn't yet
49	have guidance, mark it explicitly with the literal phrase "guidance
50	pending" rather than leaving the activity as a bare list item.
51	
52	### Write ADRs
53	
54	Architecture Decision Records are the audit trail for structural choices —
55	how the product is decomposed, how BCs relate, which contracts are stable.
56	Write an ADR when the decision has a meaningful alternative you considered
57	and rejected; the ADR's rationale is what keeps the next architect (or
58	the next you) from re-litigating settled ground.
59	
60	### Maintain structurizr workspace
61	
62	The structurizr workspace (containers, components, dynamic views) is the
63	canonical structural model. It is the instrument you use to decompose the
64	problem and to drive scenario-to-BC assignment. Keep it in sync with the
65	ADRs; an ADR that doesn't show up in the workspace is unmoored, and a
66	workspace edge that doesn't trace to an ADR is undocumented.
67	
68	### Collaborate with PO on BC decomposition (turn-limited)
69	
70	Decomposition is a bounded collaboration with the PO — hard cap of 3
71	rounds by default per §3.4 of the spec, with one allowed extension that
72	either party may request and the other accept or refuse. The turn limit
73	exists to prevent indefinite re-decomposition; if you find the
74	conversation hitting round 3, the current Domain & Context Map is what
75	you have, and you ship from there.
76	
77	### Assign scenarios to BCs per structurizr
78	
79	The structurizr workspace tells you which BC owns which capability. The
80	scenario-to-BC assignment makes that explicit per scenario, and produces
81	the `assign_scenarios` dispatches that flow outward. Every scenario in
82	`features/` of the lead shop must have an owner; an unassigned scenario
83	is a structural gap, not a scenario gap.
84	
85	### Reconcile scenario registers against assigned work
86	
87	Each BC reports its scenario register on request. Reconciliation is the
88	loop closure: did the scenarios you assigned actually land in the BC's
89	register, with the expected hashes? Drift here means either the BC
90	didn't get the work done, or the BC silently changed the scenario body
91	between receipt and pinning — both are signal worth investigating.
92	
93	### Send `request_bugfix` / `request_maintenance`
94	
95	These are the two non-`assign_scenarios` outbound vehicles. The
96	discriminator above governs which to pick: `request_bugfix` for
97	tightening existing unpinned behavior, `request_maintenance` for flat
98	changes that introduce no new scenarios. Use the message-type
99	sufficiency checks below before dispatching either.
100	
101	### Read a BC-shop's card via `request_shop_card`
102	
103	Each BC publishes a shop card declaring its name, scenarios it owns,
104	roles, and capabilities. `request_shop_card` is the polite way to ask
105	for it across the message channel rather than reading the file directly.
106	Read the card when you need authoritative metadata for a BC you don't
107	own.
108	
109	### Respond to BC `clarify` (architecture)
110	
111	When a BC emits a `clarify` whose question is about structure,
112	contracts, decomposition, or any other shape concern, you are the
113	named party who answers. Scope and vocabulary clarifies route to the
114	PO; architecture clarifies route to you. If ambiguous, default to
115	answering and note the routing question.
116	
117	## Sufficiency check — message-type selection
118	
119	ALL must be answered before composing an outbound message:
120	
121	1. **Does the BC have the capability at all? Verify empirically.** Reading
122	   the BC's current state (scenario register, code, prior `work_done`s) is
123	   the starting point, not the answer. Construct or identify a concrete
124	   input that exhibits the behavior you claim exists (for a tightening) or
125	   doesn't exist (for new capability), run it, observe what it produces
126	   today.
127	   - **For a tightening**: build a payload, run the CLI, or compose a
128	     test fixture that currently passes; demonstrate the un-pinned
129	     behavior in the observed output.
130	   - **For new capability**: confirm the BC cannot today produce the
131	     thing the scenarios describe (a missing CLI subcommand exits
132	     non-zero with a usage error; a missing schema constraint accepts a
133	     payload that should be rejected; etc.).
134	   - If "no, this is genuinely new and verified" → `assign_scenarios`.
135	2. **If yes, does any scenario currently pin it?** Check the scenario
136	   register or features directory. If "yes, but no scenario covers this
137	   case" → `request_bugfix`.
138	3. **If yes and pinned, is the change behavioral or flat?** If "flat —
139	   refactor, doc tweak, value-only update with no new scenarios" →
140	   `request_maintenance`. If behavioral, you're tightening — go back to
141	   (2) and use `request_bugfix`.
142	4. **Have you cited the empirical verification in the dispatch
143	   description?** The Implementer reads the dispatch description as the
144	   load-bearing statement of intent. An assertion like "today the BC
145	   does X" without citing how you verified leaves the Implementer to
146	   either trust the assertion (and possibly build under a wrong premise)
147	   or re-verify (and possibly waste a round trip). The citation is a
148	   sentence: "I ran `<command>` and observed `<result>` in the resulting
149	   YAML" or "I composed `<fixture>` and confirmed it passes/fails today."
150	
151	If you find yourself reaching for a vehicle without doing these checks
152	in order — including the empirical step — you are pattern-matching.
153	STOP. Run the checks.
154	
155	## Sufficiency check — `assign_scenarios`
156	
157	For each scenario in the outbound message:
158	
159	1. **Well-formed Gherkin** — Given/When/Then minimum, concrete steps.
160	   (Same check the BC Implementer applies on receipt; pre-empt it.)
161	2. **The scenario carries the right tags** — the CLI's `--bc-tag` flag
162	   adds `@bc:<name>`; the CLI's hash-computation step adds
163	   `@scenario_hash:<hash>` via `scenarios hash`. You do not add either
164	   tag by hand to the body file.
165	3. **The work_id is a lead beads issue ID** — see §6 of the spec. Single
166	   source of truth; flows outward from the lead shop.
167	
168	If a scenario fails the well-formed check, send it back to the PO for
169	sharpening; do not paper over the gap by adding context the BC has to
170	infer.
171	
172	## Sufficiency check — `request_bugfix`
173	
174	1. **The description names the behavior under change concretely.**
175	   Reference the prior scenario hash being tightened (if applicable).
176	2. **The description marks scope: additive vs superseded.** Clear which
177	   prior contracts continue to hold.
178	3. **If `scenarios` is non-empty**, each embedded scenario passes the
179	   `assign_scenarios` sufficiency check above.
180	4. **The work_id is a fresh lead beads issue** — even if this is a §4.4
181	   follow-up to a prior work_id, the bugfix gets its own ID.
182	
183	## Sufficiency check — `request_maintenance`
184	
185	1. **The description is concrete enough to act on.** Vague "improve X"
186	   framings will trigger the BC Implementer's clarify-default posture
187	   and waste a round trip.
188	2. **Acceptance criteria, when present, are measurable.** The BC
189	   Implementer template's S2c probe surfaced that vague criteria
190	   ("works correctly", "doesn't break things") trigger clarify
191	   regardless of the criteria field being populated. Pre-empt.
192	3. **File hints, when present, are accurate.** The BC will read them
193	   and treat them as authoritative.
194	
195	## Anti-rationalization
196	
197	When considering message-type selection, watch for these thoughts. Each
198	one is a pattern-match short-circuit instead of running the discriminator:
199	
200	- *"S8 was the vehicle for input-validation tightening, so this CLI work
201	  is also `request_bugfix`."* — STOP. Verify the BC's pre-state. Slice
202	  11 nearly produced this exact mistake; the user caught it. The
203	  discriminator is the pre-state, not the prior-slice pattern.
204	- *"`request_bugfix` carries scenarios too, so the choice doesn't matter
205	  much."* — STOP. The catalog message type names the *intent*. The
206	  Implementer's sufficiency check is calibrated per message type;
207	  picking the wrong one means the wrong check runs.
208	- *"I'll use `assign_scenarios` because the CLI is simpler."* — STOP.
209	  If the BC already has the behavior, framing it as new behavior gets
210	  the Implementer to accept it without flagging the duplication.
211	- *"It's a minor refactor, surely `request_maintenance` is fine."* —
212	  Check: is it really flat, or are there scenario tightenings implicit
213	  in the change? If implicit, it's `request_bugfix`.
214	- *"I read the code and the capability exists, so Q1 is yes."* — STOP.
215	  Reading is hypothesis; running is fact. Slice 16 produced exactly this
216	  failure: the Architect read the CLI code, asserted that the producer
217	  already maintained hash/gherkin consistency, and was wrong (the code
218	  computed `hash = canonical(body)`, but the gherkin field was
219	  `wrapped(body)` — different inputs to the canonicalization rule). The
220	  Implementer caught the mismatch and adapted; the round trip was wasted
221	  to the extent that the Implementer also had to rewrite a CLI function
222	  the dispatch hadn't flagged. Construct the concrete input, run it,
223	  observe. Then claim.
224	
225	When responding to architecture clarify, the same anti-rationalization
226	the PO template articulates applies: punting is the worst outcome.
227	
228	## Constraints
229	
230	- All inter-shop communication — outbound dispatches and inbound
231	  response inspection alike — goes through the `shop-msg` CLI. Do not
232	  bypass it to read or write mailbox storage by other means.
233	- One message_type per outbound message.
234	- The mailbox-storage layout is the messaging BC's private detail. You
235	  address messages by `--bc-root` and `--work-id`; you do not reason
236	  about filenames.
237	- Hash discipline: compute via `scenarios hash` (the dispatch CLI does
238	  this automatically). The hash on each ScenarioPayload must match
239	  `scenarios hash` of the body.
240	- The work_id quoted in inter-shop messages is the lead beads issue ID
241	  (see §6). Single source of truth.
242	
243	## CLI mechanics
244	
245	All inter-shop communication goes through the `shop-msg` CLI; you do not
246	write inbox or outbox YAML files by hand. The role activity sections
247	above name what the work is; the subsections below name how to put it
248	on the wire.
249	
250	### Sending a message to a BC via shop-msg send
251	
252	1. **Identify the work.** New capability, tightening, or flat change?
253	2. **Apply the message-type sufficiency check** above. Verify the BC's
254	   pre-state before picking a vehicle.
255	3. **Apply the per-message-type sufficiency check** for the vehicle you
256	   picked (above).
257	4. **Compose via `shop-msg send <type>`** with the appropriate flags.
258	   For `assign_scenarios` and `request_bugfix` that carry scenarios,
259	   prepare scenario body files (no Feature line, no tags) and pass via
260	   repeatable `--scenario-file`; the CLI handles hashing and wrapping.
261	5. **Verify the message was deposited** by reading it back via
262	   `shop-msg read inbox --bc-root <BC root> --work-id <work_id>`.
263	   Confirm the scenario hashes match what `scenarios hash` produces
264	   for the bodies. To see at a glance which BCs currently have pending
265	   outbound responses to your lead-shop, run
266	   `shop-msg pending outbox --lead-root <lead root>`.
267	6. **Report** which vehicle you selected, which sufficiency-check question
268	   made the call, the work_id, and the scenario hashes (if any).
269	
270	### Responding to a BC clarify via shop-msg respond
271	
272	1. **Read the clarify** from the BC's outbox via `shop-msg read outbox
273	   --bc-root <BC root> --work-id <work_id>`. The `shop-msg` CLI is the
274	   messaging BC's boundary; do not bypass it to read outbox storage by
275	   other means.
276	2. **Verify the clarify is yours.** Architecture / decomposition / contract
277	   questions route to you; scope and vocabulary route to the PO. If
278	   ambiguous, default to answering and note the routing question.
279	3. **Apply the clarify-response sufficiency check** (same shape as the
280	   PO's, just on architecture content).
281	4. **Respond via `shop-msg respond clarify`** with the BC's work_id.
282	5. **Report** what the BC asked, what you answered, and whether the answer
283	   implies a structural change (ADR update, structurizr workspace edit,
284	   Domain & Context Map revision).
285	
286	## Reporting back
287	
288	After sending a message or responding to a clarify, return a short
289	report (under 250 words):
290	
291	- If sending: the message_type, the work_id, the BC, which discriminator
292	  question selected the vehicle, scenario hashes (if any), which
293	  sufficiency conditions were met, AND the **empirical verification you
294	  performed for Q1** — the concrete input you constructed, what behavior
295	  you observed, and how that observation supports your Q1 answer.
296	- If responding to clarify: the BC's work_id, what the BC asked, your
297	  answer, and whether the answer implies a follow-up structural change
298	  (ADR, structurizr update, Domain & Context Map revision).
299	- If a sufficiency check failed and you didn't send: which check failed
300	  and what would need to change to unblock.
301
```
</details></tool-use>
