Now I have the full picture from the spec (§4.4): 

> The PO receives the `clarify` and decides whether to tighten the Gherkin. If the PO accepts the tightening, the revised scenario returns to the BC-shop as a **`request_bugfix`** carrying the tightened scenario text.

The lead's response to a `clarify` is NOT `shop-msg respond clarify` — that's the BC's own command. The correct flow is:
1. PO decides to accept the tightening (done — scenario file updated)
2. The revised scenario returns to the BC via `shop-msg send request_bugfix`

Let me also check if the lead-po.md's CLI mechanics instruction (step 4: `shop-msg respond clarify`) might be referring to a different command syntax, or is outdated:

The lead-po.md says:
> respond via `shop-msg respond clarify --bc-root <BC root> --work-id <work_id> --question "<your answer>"`

This appears to be an error in the lead-po.md template — it has the lead using the BC's own outbox-writing command. The spec (§4.4) is the authoritative source: the answer returns as `request_bugfix`.

However, my task description says to use `shop-msg respond clarify`. I'll follow the spec's authority (§4.4 loop description) and the actual functioning CLI over the template instruction, since the template instruction produces a collision and is architecturally incorrect.
```text
274	) -> ScenarioPayload:
275	    """Read a scenario body file, wrap it with the standard Feature header
276	    and tags, and hash the wrapped result. Shared between assign_scenarios
277	    and request_bugfix because both messages embed the same
278	    ScenarioPayload shape.
279	
280	    lead-018 tightened the ScenarioPayload schema so that
281	    `hash == canonical_hash(gherkin)`. The `gherkin` field stores the
282	    wrapped text (Feature header + tag line + body), so we must hash the
283	    wrapped text — hashing the inner body alone would put the CLI on the
284	    wrong side of the schema invariant.
285	
286	    Because the canonicalization rule strips `@scenario_hash:` lines
287	    before hashing, the chicken-and-egg of "the tag line includes the
288	    hash we're about to compute" resolves naturally: we can construct
289	    the wrapped text using the eventual hash as a placeholder, hash it,
290	    then replace the placeholder — but the simpler form below works
291	    because the canonicalization strips `@scenario_hash:<anything>`
292	    lines unconditionally. So we build the wrapped text with a sentinel
293	    hash, compute the canonical hash of that string (the sentinel line
294	    is dropped by canonicalization), and emit the same wrapped string
295	    with the now-known hash substituted in. The substitution does not
296	    perturb the canonical hash because both forms differ only in the
297	    stripped `@scenario_hash:` line.
298	    """
299	    body = Path(path_str).read_text()
300	    sentinel = "0" * 16
301	    sentinel_tags = [f"@scenario_hash:{sentinel}", f"@bc:{bc_tag}"]
302	    sentinel_tagged = (
303	        f"Feature: {feature_title}\n"
304	        f"\n"
305	        f"  {' '.join(sentinel_tags)}\n"
306	        f"  {body}\n"
307	    )
308	    scen_hash = _compute_scenario_hash(sentinel_tagged)
309	    tags = [f"@scenario_hash:{scen_hash}", f"@bc:{bc_tag}"]
310	    tagged = (
311	        f"Feature: {feature_title}\n"
312	        f"\n"
313	        f"  {' '.join(tags)}\n"
314	        f"  {body}\n"
315	    )
316	    return ScenarioPayload(hash=scen_hash, tags=tags, gherkin=tagged)
317	
318	
319	def _cmd_send_request_bugfix(args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
320	    bc_root = str(Path(args.bc_root).resolve())
321	
322	    scenario_files = list(args.scenario_file or [])
323	    # --feature-title and --bc-tag are conditionally required: only when
324	    # at least one --scenario-file is supplied. argparse cannot express
325	    # "required iff" directly, so enforce post-parse here.
326	    if scenario_files and (args.feature_title is None or args.bc_tag is None):
327	        print(
328	            "shop-msg send request_bugfix: --feature-title and --bc-tag are "
```

```text
Issues chained together like beads. A lightweight issue tracker with first-class dependency support.

Usage:
  bd [flags]
  bd [command]

Working With Issues:
  assign          Assign an issue to someone
  children        List child beads of a parent
  close           Close one or more issues
  comment         Add a comment to an issue
  comments        View or manage comments on an issue
  create          Create a new issue (or batch from markdown/graph JSON)
  create-form     Create a new issue using an interactive form
  delete          Delete one or more issues and clean up references
  edit            Edit an issue field in $EDITOR
  gate            Manage async coordination gates
  label           Manage issue labels
  link            Link two issues with a dependency
  list            List issues
  merge-slot      Manage merge-slot gates for serialized conflict resolution
  note            Append a note to an issue
  priority        Set the priority of an issue
  promote         Promote a wisp to a permanent bead
  q               Quick capture: create issue and output only ID
  query           Query issues using a simple query language
  reopen          Reopen one or more closed issues
  search          Search issues by text query
  set-state       Set operational state (creates event + updates label)
  show            Show issue details
```

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-19 15:37:09Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>
Run bd prime to see session context and commands

`cd /workspaces/shopsystem-product && bd prime 2>&1 | head -50`

```text
# Beads Workflow Context

> **Context Recovery**: Run `bd prime` after compaction, clear, or new session
> Hooks auto-call this in Claude Code when a beads workspace is resolved

# 🚨 SESSION CLOSE PROTOCOL 🚨

**CRITICAL**: Before saying "done" or "complete", you MUST run this checklist:

```
[ ] 1. git status              (check what changed)
[ ] 2. git add <files>         (stage code changes)
[ ] 3. git commit -m "..."     (commit code)
[ ] 4. git push                (push to remote)
```

**NEVER skip this.** Work is not done until pushed.

## Core Rules
- **Default**: Use beads for ALL task tracking (`bd create`, `bd ready`, `bd close`)
- **Prohibited**: Do NOT use TodoWrite, TaskCreate, or markdown files for task tracking
- **Workflow**: Create beads issue BEFORE writing code, mark in_progress when starting
- **Memory**: Use `bd remember "insight"` for persistent knowledge across sessions. Do NOT use MEMORY.md files — they fragment across accounts. Search with `bd memories <keyword>`.
- Persistence you don't need beats lost context
- Git workflow: beads auto-commit to Dolt, run `git push` at session end
- Session management: check `bd ready` for available work

## Essential Commands

### Finding Work
- `bd ready` - Show issues ready to work (no blockers)
- `bd list --status=open` - All open issues
- `bd list --status=in_progress` - Your active work
- `bd show <id>` - Detailed issue view with dependencies

### Creating & Updating
- `bd create --title="Summary of this issue" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task|bug|feature --priority=2` - New issue
  - Priority: 0-4 or P0-P4 (0=critical, 2=medium, 4=backlog). NOT "high"/"medium"/"low"
- `bd update <id> --claim` - Claim work
- `bd update <id> --assignee=username` - Assign to someone
- `bd update <id> --title/--description/--notes/--design` - Update fields inline
- `bd close <id>` - Mark complete
- `bd close <id1> <id2> ...` - Close multiple issues at once (more efficient)
- `bd close <id> --reason="explanation"` - Close with reason
- **Tip**: When creating multiple issues/tasks/epics, use parallel subagents for efficiency
- **WARNING**: Do NOT use `bd edit` - it opens $EDITOR (vim/nano) which blocks agents

### Dependencies & Blocking
- `bd dep add <issue> <depends-on>` - Add dependency (issue depends on depends-on)
- `bd blocked` - Show all blocked issues
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-19 15:38:46Z)**_

Everything is done. Here is the summary:

---

**PO clarify-response report — work_id: lead-k98**

**What the BC asked:** The Then step in scenario `bf94500ef1636ce2` (`15-watch-live-notification.gherkin`) only verifies the lower bound — that at least one line containing the work_id arrives. A buggy implementation emitting the same event twice would still pass. For a Monitor consumer, duplicate events trigger duplicate processing. The BC Reviewer proposed: `And no additional output line arrives within 2 seconds`.

**Decision:** Accepted. The "at most one" bound is load-bearing; the scenario must pin both bounds.

**Lead-shop scenario file updated:** `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/features/messaging/15-watch-live-notification.gherkin`
- Added step: `And no additional output line arrives within 2 seconds`
- Added tag: `@scenario_hash:6b5910b7b30777d8`
- Old hash: `bf94500ef1636ce2` (the scenario previously lacked a hash tag)
- New canonical hash: **`6b5910b7b30777d8`**

**Wire message sent:** `shop-msg send request_bugfix` dispatched to `shopsystem-messaging` as work_id **`lead-uqv`** (new beads issue), carrying the tightened scenario. This follows the §4.4 loop: PO accepts → `request_bugfix` with tightened scenario text → BC re-implements and Reviewer re-gates.

**Note on `shop-msg respond clarify`:** The lead-po.md template instructs using this command, but it is the BC's own outbox command (for BC→lead questions). _**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-19 15:44:01Z)**_

Significant progress. Here's the summary:

**shopsystem-messaging:**
- `lead-8yi` ✅ — compose.yml updated: `./data/pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data` host-bind, `shopsystem` network with `attachable: true`
- `lead-16g` ✅ — `shop-msg prime --bc-root PATH` implemented; prints DSN, reachability, pending inbox, and CLI reminder

**shopsystem-templates:**
- `lead-3c6` ✅ — 3 scenarios retired (bd prime, host prereqs, Monitor activation strings no longer asserted on CLAUDE.md body)
- `lead-ro8` ✅ — **Import graph fully implemented**: 15 scenarios passing, 4 typed files written at bootstrap (`.claude/shop/name.md`, `.claude/shop/type.md`, `.claude/canonical/<type>-primer.md`, `.claude/shop/primer.md`), `update` command updated to read shop type from `.claude/shop/type.md`
- `lead-7yh` ✅ — `shop-msg prime` wired as BC SessionStart hook in `bc.json`

**Mechanism observation (needs action):** The templates BC caught a bug — `bc.json` sets the hook command as `shop-msg prime` (no `--bc-root`), but `--bc-root` is a required flag. Every BC bootstrapped from this template gets a broken SessionStart hook. Proposed fix: change to `shop-msg prime --bc-root .`. That's a `request_maintenance` — want me to dispatch it now?

1. **Inbox/outbox protocol section** still describes `inbox/*.yaml` filesystem files — wrong since postgres
2. **Session start section** still instructs `inotifywait -m inbox/` via Monitor — inoperative with no filesystem inbox

The BC's proposed fix is polling (`shop-msg pending inbox`), but that's interim — `shop-msg watch` (lead-k98) is the right Monitor pipeline once it lands. Dispatching `request_maintenance` with the correct spec.

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>
Read the lead-k98 mechanism_observation

`shop-msg dump --bc-root /workspaces/shopsystem-product/repos/shopsystem-messaging --direction outbox 2>&1 | grep -A 30 "lead-k98" | grep -A 25 "mechanism_observation"`

```text
message_type: mechanism_observation
payload:
  body: "## Observation\n\n`psycopg.Connection.notifies()` acquires `conn.lock` (a\
    \ non-reentrant `threading.Lock`)\nat its `with self.lock:` entry and holds it\
    \ for the lifetime of the generator \u2014 including\nacross `yield` suspensions.\
    \  Any subsequent use of the same connection inside the `for`\nloop body (e.g.\
    \ `conn.cursor().execute(...)`) attempts to re-acquire the same lock on the\n\
    same thread, causing an immediate deadlock.\n\nThe original `watch_inbox` implementation\
    \ in storage.py did exactly this: it called\n`with conn.cursor(row_factory=dict_row)\
    \ as cur: cur.execute(...)` on the LISTEN\nconnection inside the `for notify in\
    \ conn.notifies():` loop to look up the message_type.\nThe NOTIFY was received\
    \ by psycopg (confirmed via stderr tracing), but the process hung\nsilently because\
    \ the lock could never be re-acquired.\n\n## Why it's load-bearing\n\nAny future\
    \ implementation that uses a psycopg synchronous LISTEN connection for\nauxiliary\
    \ queries (schema checks, message lookups, cursor iteration) inside a `notifies()`\n\
    loop will silently deadlock \u2014 there is no exception, no log message, and\
    \ the process\nappears alive. This is a subtle invariant that is easy to violate\
    \ in refactors.\n\n## Fix applied\n\nUse `_connect()` (a separate connection)\
    \ for the message_type lookup inside the\n`conn.notifies()` loop. This is the\
    \ correct pattern for all DB operations performed\nwhile `notifies()` is active\
    \ on a given connection.\n\n## Secondary observation (subprocess pipe buffering)\n\
    \n`select.select([proc.stdout.buffer], ...)` on a `BufferedReader` fd checks the\
    \ kernel\npipe fd, but `BufferedReader.read1()` may have already drained data\
    \ from the kernel fd\ninto its internal buffer. Subsequent select calls then report\
    \ \"not readable\" even though\ndata is available via `buffer.read1()`. The fix\
    \ is to use the raw `FileIO` layer\n(`proc.stdout.buffer.raw`) which has no internal\
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-19 16:39:53Z)**_

No `work_done` yet — `lead-uqv` (the tightened scenario) is in the inbox and the BC is still working through it.

The mechanism_observation is substantive though — the BC hit a real psycopg deadlock during implementation:

- **psycopg `notifies()` deadlock**: `conn.notifies()` holds `conn.lock` for the lifetime of the generator, including across `yield` suspensions. Any query on the *same* connection inside the loop deadlocks silently. Fix: use a separate connection (`_connect()`) for the message_type lookup inside the `notifies()` loop.

- **Secondary: subprocess pipe buffering**: `select.select` on a `BufferedReader` fd misses data already drained into the internal buffer. Fix: use the raw `FileIO` layer (`proc.stdout.buffer.raw`) which has no internal buffer.

Both are fixed in the implementation — the observation is filed so future refactors don't reintroduce the deadlock. The BC is still working; `work_done` on `lead-k98`/`lead-uqv` hasn't come back yet.

```text
  \ deadline loop with select.select runs until deadline; (2) race where same message\
  \ emitted twice by drain+NOTIFY? Not possible: LISTEN is registered before drain,\
  \ but any message present at drain time was already in DB before LISTEN was set\
  \ up, so its NOTIFY was lost; only messages inserted after LISTEN fires a NOTIFY;\
```

```bash
  --description "Supersedes lead-5r0. shop-msg watch (lead-k98) is now complete and available. Update src/shop_templates/templates/claude/bc.md to replace two obsolete sections. Mechanism_observation shopsystem-templates-hv4 (observed during lead-5hy) identified both.

SECTION 1 — BC inbox/outbox protocol:
Replace the filesystem YAML description (inbox/*.yaml, outbox/<work_id>-<type>.yaml) with the postgres shop-msg CLI model: inbox and outbox are in postgres. Key commands: 'shop-msg pending inbox --bc-root .' to list unprocessed messages; 'shop-msg read inbox --bc-root . --work-id <id>' to read a specific message; 'shop-msg respond ...' to write outbox responses. Do not reference YAML files or filesystem paths for inbox/outbox.

SECTION 2 — Session start:
Replace the inotifywait/stdbuf Monitor section entirely with:
  1. 'shop-msg prime --bc-root .' — orientation: DSN reachability, pending inbox count, CLI reminder. Run at session start.
  2. 'bd prime' — beads workflow context. Run at session start.
  3. Monitor activation: arm Monitor on 'shop-msg watch --bc-root .' — this is the postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY watcher that outputs one line per new inbox message, usable directly as a Claude Code Monitor pipeline. shop-msg watch is now available (shopsystem-messaging lead-k98/lead-uqv). It replaces inotifywait entirely.
Remove all references to inotifywait, stdbuf, host-prereq checks for those tools, and 'refuse to arm the Monitor / surface a diagnostic if missing' language. The shop-msg watch command handles DB-unreachable fail-fast itself." \
  --acceptance-criterion "bc.md contains no reference to inbox/*.yaml or outbox/*.yaml filesystem paths" \
  --acceptance-criterion "bc.md contains no reference to inotifywait or stdbuf" \
  --acceptance-criterion "bc.md session-start section names shop-msg prime --bc-root . and shop-msg watch --bc-root . as the definitive activation pattern" \
  --acceptance-criterion "bc.md names no host-level prerequisites for inotify-tools or coreutils" \
  --file-hint "src/shop_templates/templates/claude/bc.md" 2>&1
```

```text
message_type: work_done
```

```text
status: blocked
summary: 'Scenario 3 Then step ''shop-msg pending outbox reports the new work_id as
  pending for the lead shop'' is implemented by calling ''shop-msg pending inbox --bc-root
  <bc-root>'' instead of ''shop-msg pending outbox --lead-root <lead-root>''. These
  are different CLI surfaces with different semantics: pending inbox is BC-side (shows
  inbox items with no outbox response), pending outbox is lead-side (scans repos/
  subdirs for BC outbox responses). The scenario explicitly names the lead-shop-facing
  ''pending outbox'' surface. The test passes because pending inbox also exits zero
  when the message was sent successfully, but any regression in the pending outbox
  CLI path or its --lead-root/repos/ contract would not be caught. The step must be
  rewritten to invoke shop-msg pending outbox with a lead-root structure that reflects
  the container scenario.'
scenario_hashes: []
```
</details></tool-use>
